1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, the entire monoidal scaffolding for (3) is already
88	available abstractly, and the project's task is to \emph{instantiate} it rather
89	than to hand-assemble the coherence data. Two facts drive this. First, the
90	category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a
91	presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric) monoidal category in
92	Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)), with the sectionwise
93	relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)\); this applies
94	verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second,
95	Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\) --- associator,
100	left/right unitors, braiding, and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
101	naturalities) derived. Sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the
102	localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class
103	\(W = J.W\), so the monoidal structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is
104	\emph{free} from this API once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) is supplied.
105	
106	Consequently the only genuinely-new obligation is the single instance
107	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) for the sheafification localizer on
108	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\). Its two fields are the
109	left/right whiskering-stability statements \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(F \triangleleft
110	g)\) and \(J.W\,g \Rightarrow J.W(g \triangleright F)\) for an \emph{arbitrary}
111	module \(F\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), and these are flatness-free: a
112	\(J.W\)-morphism is a stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x =
113	\mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an isomorphism (tensoring with an
114	isomorphism preserves isomorphisms --- no flatness, no local triviality). The
115	load-bearing half is the local-injectivity statement
116	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, whose stalkwise proof needs two
117	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\)-level ingredients that are Mathlib-absent at the
118	pinned commit (the module-level stalk characterisation of \(J.W\), and the
119	commutation of the stalk with the relative tensor); this is the
120	\emph{sole remaining open obligation} for the whole group-law engine.
121	
122	With \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) in hand, the substrate \(\otimes_X\) carries the
123	associator, unitors and braiding from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), and the
124	group law assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units
125	group is the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), exactly as
126	Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as
127	\(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\). The
128	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
129	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse
130	existentially. The geometric compatibility isomorphism
131	\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any
132	separate tensor-inverse lemma are \emph{off} this critical path; see also
133	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

265	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
266	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
267	
268	\begin{definition}
269	
270	\leanok
271	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
272	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
274	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
275	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
276	  \[
277	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
278	  \]
279	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
280	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
281	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
282	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
283	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
284	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
285	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
286	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
287	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
288	  canonical compatibility
289	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
290	     \;\cong\;
291	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
292	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
293	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
294	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
295	
296	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
297	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
298	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
299	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
300	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
301	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
302	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
303	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
304	\end{definition}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}
307	
308	\leanok
309	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
310	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
311	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
312	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
313	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
314	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
315	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
316	  \[
317	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
318	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
319	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
320	  \]
321	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
322	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
323	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
324	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
325	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
326	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
327	  \[
328	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
329	    \qquad
330	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
331	  \]
332	  the associator
333	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
334	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
335	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
336	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
337	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
342	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
343	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
344	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
345	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
346	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
347	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
348	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
349	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
350	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
351	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
352	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
353	\end{proof}
354	
355	\begin{remark}
356	[The full monoidal category is cheap via route~(e); the group law consumes only propositions]
357	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
358	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
359	        lem:whisker_of_W}
360	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
361	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
362	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
363	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
364	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
365	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. Under route~(e) this is
366	  \emph{cheap}, not expensive: it is exactly the output of
367	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
368	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
369	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), once the single instance
370	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}) is supplied. All the
371	  coherence is then derived by the API.
372	
373	  Crucially, \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) needs \emph{no} closed structure. Its
374	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
375	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
376	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
377	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
378	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\) (tensoring with an isomorphism
379	  preserves isomorphisms). This is the same technique Mathlib uses for
380	  fixed-base presheaves in
381	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\), ported to
382	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). It uses neither
383	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
384	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
385	  false over a non-affine open and is therefore off the critical path); the
386	  earlier reading that arbitrary-\(F\) whisker stability requires
387	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) was the \emph{wrong altitude}.
388	
389	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
390	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
391	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
392	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
393	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
394	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
395	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
396	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
397	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
398	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, all now supplied by the route~(e) monoidal structure;
399	  none of them is built from the off-path restriction-compatibility isomorphism
400	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
401	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
402	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
403	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
404	  over a fixed ring, here over the sheafification localizer via
405	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\).
406	\end{remark}

408	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
409	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
410	
411	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
412	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
413	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
414	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
415	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
416	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
417	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes ---
418	under route~(e) these are read off the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure of
419	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} --- together with the closure of \(\otimes\) on the
420	locally-trivial \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype
421	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and the interaction with the pullback
422	functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
423	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is retained as an off-path supplement, not as a
424	group-law ingredient; cf.\ \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
425	
426	\begin{lemma}
427	
428	\leanok
429	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
430	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
431	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
432	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
433	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
434	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
435	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
436	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
437	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
438	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
439	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
440	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
441	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
442	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
443	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
444	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
445	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
446	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
447	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
448	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
449	  \[
450	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
451	      \;\;:=\;\;
452	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
453	  \]
454	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
455	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
456	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
457	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
458	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
459	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
460	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
461	\end{lemma}
462	
463	\begin{proof}
464	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
465	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
466	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
467	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
468	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
469	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
470	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf

494	\begin{lemma}
495	
496	\leanok
497	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
498	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
499	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
500	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
501	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
502	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
503	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
504	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
505	  morphism
506	  \[
507	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
508	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
509	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
510	  \]
511	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
512	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
513	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
514	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
515	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
516	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
517	\end{lemma}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
521	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
522	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
523	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
524	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
525	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
526	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
527	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
528	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
529	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
530	  isomorphism}
531	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
532	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
533	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
534	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
535	  therefore extension of scalars along
536	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
537	
538	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
539	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
540	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
541	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
542	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
543	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
544	  the abstract pullback functor.
545	
546	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
547	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
548	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
549	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
550	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
551	   \;\cong\;
552	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
553	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
554	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
555	  comparison
556	  \[
557	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
558	      \;\longrightarrow\;
559	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
560	      \;\otimes\;
561	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
562	  \]
563	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
564	
565	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
566	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
567	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
568	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
569	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
570	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
571	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
572	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
573	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
574	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
575	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
576	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
577	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
578	  coherence isomorphisms
579	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
580	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
581	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
582	\end{proof}

587	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
588	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
589	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
590	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

611	\begin{lemma}
612	
613	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
614	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
615	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerLeft_of_flat, PresheafOfModules.W_whiskerRight_of_flat}
616	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
617	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
618	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
619	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
620	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
621	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
622	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
623	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
624	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
625	  left-whiskered morphism
626	  \[
627	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
628	  \]
629	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
630	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
631	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
632	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
633	
634	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
635	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
636	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
637	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
638	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
639	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
640	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
641	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
642	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
643	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
644	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
645	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
646	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
647	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
648	\end{lemma}
649	
650	\begin{proof}
651	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
652	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
653	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
654	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
655	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
656	
657	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
658	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
659	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
660	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
661	  \[
662	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
663	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
664	  \]
665	
666	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
667	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
668	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
669	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
670	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
671	
672	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
673	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
674	  linear maps (Mathlib
675	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
676	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
677	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
678	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
679	
680	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
681	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
682	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
683	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
684	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
685	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
686	\end{proof}

1281	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1282	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1283	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1284	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1285	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1286	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1287	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1288	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1289	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1290	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1291	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1292	  01CR).
1293	
1294	  Route~(e) supplies this directly. By \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} the category
1295	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
1296	  is a (symmetric) \(\MonoidalCategory\) with tensor \(\otimes_X\) and unit
1297	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), obtained from \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\).
1298	  Its set of isomorphism classes --- the skeleton \(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.%
1299	  \mathtt{Modules}\,X)\) --- is therefore a commutative monoid under
1300	  \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\): the multiplication is well-defined because
1301	  \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality});
1302	  the unit is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and associativity, unitality and commutativity
1303	  are the existence of the coherence isomorphisms
1304	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1305	  read as equalities of iso-classes. The Picard group is then the group of
1306	  \emph{units} of this commutative monoid,
1307	  \[
1308	    \Pic(X)
1309	      \;\;=\;\;
1310	    \mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X)\bigr),
1311	  \]
1312	  exactly as Mathlib builds
1313	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}
1314	  \,R))\) and transports \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). A class \([M]\) is a unit
1315	  precisely when \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
1316	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse being carried
1317	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\). The pinned carrier
1318	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) is this commutative monoid of all
1319	  iso-classes; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at

1332	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:jw_ismonoidal,

1354	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
1355	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
1356	
1357	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
1358	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
1359	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
1360	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1361	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
1362	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
1363	
1364	\begin{theorem}
1365	
1366	\leanok
1367	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
1368	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
1370	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1371	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1372	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1373	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1374	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1375	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
1376	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
1377	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1378	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1379	
1380	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
1381	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
1382	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
1383	  % groups)
1384	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
1385	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1386	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1387	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1388	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1389	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
1390	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1391	  % \end{dfn}
1392	  %  [\dots]
1393	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1394	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1395	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1396	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1397	  % topologies  by
1398	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1399	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1400	  %  \end{dfn}"
1401	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1402	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1403	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1404	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1405	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1406	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1407	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1408	  \[
1409	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1410	  \]
1411	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1412	  abelian-group structure with addition
1413	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1414	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1415	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1416	  inverse
1417	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1418	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1419	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1420	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1421	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1422	  map
1423	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1424	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1425	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1426	\end{theorem}
1427	
1428	\begin{proof}
1429	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1430	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1431	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1432	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1433	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1434	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1435	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1436	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1437	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1438	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1439	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1440	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1441	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1442	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1443	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1444	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1445	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1446	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1447	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1448	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1449	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1450	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1451	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1452	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1453	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1454	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1455	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1456	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1457	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1458	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1459	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1460	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1461	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1462	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1463	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1464	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1465	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1466	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1467	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1468	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1469	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1470	
1471	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1472	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1473	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1474	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1475	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1476	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1477	
1478	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1479	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1480	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1481	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1482	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1483	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1484	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1485	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1486	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1487	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1488	\end{proof}

1274	\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1275	 The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1276	 category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1277	abelian groups defined by the formula
1278		$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1279	\end{dfn}
1280	
1281	The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
1282	sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
1283		$$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$
1284	 Hence, given a covering family $\{T_i\to T\}$ in the Zariski topology,
1285	the \'etale topology, or any other Grothendieck topology on the category
1286	of $S$-schemes, there is no harm, when we consider the sheaf associated
1287	to $\Pic_X$, in working simply with the single map $T'\to T$ where
1288	$T':=\coprod T_i$.  And doing so lightens the notation, making for
1289	easier reading. Therefore, we do so throughout, calling $T'\to T$ simply
1290	a {\it covering\/} in the given topology.
1291	
1292	The absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never a separated presheaf in
1293	the Zariski topology.  Indeed, take an $S$-scheme $T$ that carries an
1294	invertible sheaf $\mc N$ such that $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.  (For
1295	example, take $T:=\I P^1_X$ and $\mc N:=\mc O_{\!T}(1)$.  Then the diagonal
1296	map $X\to X\x X$ induces a section $g$ of $f_T$; that is, $gf_T=1_T$.
1297	Hence $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.)  Now, there exists a Zariski covering
1298	$T'\to T$ such that the pullback $\mc N|T'$ is trivial; here, $T'$ is
1299	simply the disjoint union of the subsets in a suitable ordinary open
1300	covering of $T$.  So the pullback $f^*_{T'}\mc N|X_{T'}$ is trivial too.
1301	Thus $\Pic_X(T)$ has a nonzero element whose restriction is zero in
1302	$\Pic_X(T')$.
1303	
1304	According to descent theory, every representable functor is a sheaf in
1305	the Zariski topology \emdash in fact, in the \'etale and fppf
1306	topologies as well.  Therefore, the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never
1307	representable.  So, in the hope of obtaining a representable functor
1308	that differs as little as possible from $\Pic_X$, we now define a
1309	sequence of three successively more promising relative functors.
